title: Redeploying dirty assets: The impact of environmental
abstract: This paper investigates how firms’ pollution incentives are influenced by their ability to divest polluted assets. My empirical setting is a major reform that exempts purchasers from liability for past contamination. Using a difference-in-differences framework, I find that the reform reduces toxic emissions, lowers bankruptcy risk, and increases firm value. Cross-sectional tests show that the decline in emissions is driven by firms with weaker financial health and fewer assets. These findings highlight a novel net worth channel: by limiting ex-post liability, the reform enhances landowners’ net worth ex-ante, reducing their incentives to engage in risky behavior, such as excessive emissions.

author.Jason Chen: Harbert College of Business, Auburn University, 405 W Magnolia Ave, Auburn, 36830, United States

article.1. Introduction: Corporations are under growing pressure to divest polluting assets, with major firms offloading billions of dollars worth of high-emission assets in response to rising demands for climate action.1 While policies designed to improve the liquidity of polluting assets may facilitate such divestitures, their environmental implications remain unclear. Recent research suggests that these policies might be ineffective, as emissions are transferred from sellers to buyers following the divestiture of polluting assets (Duchin et al., 2024). However, understanding the full environmental impact of such policies requires examining their ex-ante effects — how firms adjust their environmental behavior when they anticipate being able to sell polluting assets more easily or at higher prices. In this paper, I address this gap by investigating the environmental implications of improving the liquidity of polluting assets, with a particular focus on their ex-ante effects.Estimating the environmental effects of improved liquidity poses two challenges: first, accurately measuring the liquidity of brown assets, and second, addressing potential correlations between this measure and unobservable firm characteristics that influence environmental behavior. To overcome these challenges, I exploit the passage of the Brownfields Act in 2002 as a natural experiment. This reform facilitated industrial land transactions by exempting purchasers from prior cleanup liabilities and disproportionately impacting certain firms, enabling a difference-in-differences research design. I find that, in response to the reform, affected landowners reduce toxic emissions, experience a decline in bankruptcy risk, and observe an increase in firm value. I attribute these effects to the reform increasing landowners’ net worth, thereby reducing their incentive to take risks through higher emissions. These findings suggest that while policies promoting divestiture might be ineffective ex-post, they can generate positive environmental outcomes ex-ante, especially when driven by reductions in purchaser liability.In the United States, land purchasers can inherit environmental liabilities, becoming responsible for contamination caused by previous owners after a transaction. These liabilities are often substantial and unpredictable, with cleanup costs sometimes reaching billions of dollars (Eisen, 1996, Greenstone and Gallagher, 2008). As a result, purchasers have been hesitant to acquire industrial land, leading to an illiquid land market where many industrial sites remain abandoned (Ramseur, 2008, Hayes and Dinkin, 1988). To address this issue, Congress enacted the Small Business Liability Relief and Brownfields Revitalization Act (Brownfields Act hereafter) in 2002, which reduced purchasers’ exposure to cleanup liabilities caused by previous owners.I utilize the Brownfields Act as an empirical setting, exploiting its unique features to construct treated and control groups. In the U.S., environmental cleanups are governed by two primary statutes: the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA) and the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA). A key aspect of the Brownfields Act is that it only exempts purchasers from liability under CERCLA (EPA, 2022). As a result, plants regulated solely by CERCLA are particularly affected by the reform. In contrast, plants regulated by both CERCLA and RCRA serve as a natural comparison group, as the risk of incurring RCRA cleanup costs continues to deter purchasers (Burnham-Howard, 2004). Leveraging this institutional detail, I classify plants regulated solely by CERCLA as the treated group and RCRA-regulated plants as the control group.I perform three distinct tests to validate this classification. First, I examine whether treated plants exhibit higher exposure to CERCLA prior to the reform, as this would indicate that their purchasers benefited more from the reform’s liability protection. Consistent with this, I find that the EPA is more likely to use CERCLA to address contamination at treated plants compared to control plants prior to the reform. Second, I show that treated plants incurred almost no RCRA cleanups prior to the reform, indicating that RCRA liabilities had minimal impact on their redeployment. Finally, I demonstrate that, following the reform, the treated group increased its propensity to sell assets relative to the control group. These results further validate my treatment classification, as they align with existing research showing that reducing purchaser liability enhances the liquidity of industrial land (Sigman, 2010, Howland, 2000, McGrath, 2000).After validating my empirical design, I analyze the reform’s impact on industrial pollution. Theoretically, the reform’s effect on emissions is ambiguous. On the one hand, reducing purchasers’ liability increases landowners’ net worth by enhancing the value and liquidity of their land. This increased net worth mitigates incentives to take risks through excessive pollution, a mechanism I term the net worth channel. Additionally, if total cleanup liability remains fixed, reducing purchasers’ liability shifts a greater burden onto the current landowner, potentially leading to lower emissions, as the current owner would bear a larger share of the cleanup costs in the event of contamination. I refer to this as the liability-shifting channel. On the other hand, the reform could also lead to higher emissions by enabling firms to sell treated plants more easily and at higher prices, even when they become contaminated.I use granular data from the EPA’s Toxic Release Inventory (TRI) database to analyze the environmental effects of the Brownfields Act. Treatment is defined at both the plant and firm levels, with firms classified as treated if the majority of their plants are affected by the reform. My most stringent specification includes chemical-year, plant-chemical, firm, industry-year, and state-year fixed effects. This approach identifies the effect of the Brownfields Act by comparing emissions from plants within the same industry, using the same chemical, in the same state, but with different levels of exposure to the reform. I find that treated firms reduce emissions by approximately 7% compared to control firms following the Brownfields Act. While treated plants also exhibit some reduction in emissions, the effects are more modest, with point estimates indicating a decline between 2.8% and 4.1%, accompanied by weak statistical significance. In a “horse race” specification that includes both plant and firm treatment variables, the results indicate that the reduction in emissions is driven entirely by firm-wide decreases. Although both the net worth and liability-shifting channels suggest a decline in emissions, the firm-wide reduction is more consistent with the net worth channel. This is because an increase in a firm’s net worth reduces emissions across all plants, whereas the shift in liability affects only the treated plants.To further distinguish between the two mechanisms, I conduct several additional analyses. First, I examine the reform’s impact on net worth, a variable for which the two mechanisms yield contrasting predictions. The net worth channel suggests that the reform positively affects the net worth of treated landowners by increasing land value, while the liability-shifting channel predicts that transferring purchaser liability to landowners would negatively impact their net worth. I find evidence consistent with the net worth channel. After the reform, treated firms exhibit improved financial health, as indicated by increases in Altman’s Z score, and higher firm value, measured by Tobin’s Q. These improvements in net worth are economically significant, with Altman’s Z score and Tobin’s Q increasing by approximately 10% to 15% relative to their sample mean.Next, I perform cross-sectional tests to further pinpoint the underlying mechanisms. The net worth mechanism, which reduces emissions by mitigating incentives to take risks through higher emissions, should have the greatest impact on firms where pollution-shifting incentives are strongest — such as firms with high bankruptcy risk or fewer assets. In contrast, the liability-shifting mechanism has a smaller effect on these firms, as liabilities transferred from purchasers are more likely to be discharged in bankruptcy. The evidence shows that emissions reductions are more significant among firms with stronger pollution-shifting incentives, providing further support for the net worth channel.Finally, I examine the reform’s impact on economic activity. Since real estate can serve as collateral, an increase in land value should alleviate the financing constraints of landowners, thereby boosting their economic activity (Ersahin and Irani, 2020, Chaney et al., 2012). Additionally, improved liquidity in the land market facilitates a more efficient allocation of resources, enhancing the productivity of landowners (Gavazza, 2011). Thus, if the net worth channel drives the results, we should observe increased economic activity following the reform. Conversely, a negative effect on economic activity would support the liability-shifting mechanism, where redirected liability raises the cost of capital for landowners and reduces their economic activity. Using plant-chemical-level production data from the EPA and plant-level employment data from the National Establishment Time Series (NETS), I find that the reform has a modest but positive impact on economic activity. These findings are consistent with the net worth channel and contradict the liability-shifting mechanism.This paper contributes to the literature on the divestiture of brown assets. Prior research shows that the divestment of financial assets, such as equity and debt, is generally an ineffective strategy for improving social outcomes and may even be counterproductive (Edmans and Levit, 2022, Berk and Van Binsbergen, 2025, Broccardo et al., 2022, Becht et al., 2023, Kahn et al., 2023, Kacperczyk and Peydró, 2022, Sachdeva et al., 2023, Heath et al., 2023, Green and Roth, 2024, Hartzmark and Shue, 2023, Bellon and Boualam, 2024). An exception to this occurs when there is an inelastic supply of capital, making it difficult for firms targeted by divestiture campaigns to substitute into alternative sources of financing (Green and Vallee, 2024). In contrast, research on real asset divestitures remains limited. Duchin et al. (2024) and Berg et al. (2003) show that when firms are pressured to improve their environmental performance, they often sell their polluting assets to buyers facing less environmental scrutiny. Duchin et al. (2024) further track emissions from divested plants and find no significant reduction, interpreting this as evidence of greenwashing, where firms divest dirty assets to appear cleaner without achieving meaningful environmental improvements.This paper contributes to the literature in three key ways. First, I demonstrate that divestitures policies that increase the demand for brown assets can yield positive social outcomes, particularly in the context of reducing purchaser liability. Second, I show that such divestitures are effective because they enhance the net worth of current owners, thereby reducing their incentives to pollute. This contrasts with policies that encourage the divestiture of financial assets, which may negatively impact the target firm’s financial health and worsen environmental behavior (Hartzmark and Shue, 2023, Bellon and Boualam, 2024). Third, to my knowledge, this paper is the first to explore the ex-ante effects of divestiture policies. Given the increasing interest in tackling environmental issues, understanding such ex-ante effects helps inform policymakers on how to design effective divestiture policies.This paper also contributes to the literature on environmental liability. This line of work studies the consequences of imposing environmental liability on shareholders and lenders (Akey and Appel, 2021, Bellon, 2024b, Bellon, 2024a, Boomhower, 2019, Ohlrogge, 2024). I extend this literature by investigating the implications of purchaser liability, an underexplored liability regime. Unlike shareholders and creditors, purchasers do not directly control firm policies. A reduction in purchaser liability affects emissions through the industrial land market, leading to a decrease in emissions. In contrast, loosening liability for shareholders and creditors worsens corporate environmental behavior. Thus, my finding highlights a rare case in which a reduction in environmental liability reduces emissions.Finally, this paper deepens our understanding of environmental regulations. The prevailing view is that loosening these regulations yields economic benefits (e.g., increased employment) but comes at the expense of environmental outcomes (e.g., Akey and Appel, 2021, Becker and Henderson, 2000, Bellon, 2024b, Greenstone et al., 2012, Walker, 2013). My findings, however, suggest that this trade-off does not always apply. In particular, reducing purchaser liability improves land liquidity and increases landowners’ net worth without adversely affecting environmental behavior.
article.2. Institutional background: 2.1. Background on environmental cleanupIn the U.S., environmental cleanups are governed by two statutes: the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA) and the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA).CERCLA, enacted in 1980, was designed to fund the cleanup of contaminated sites by granting the EPA authority to impose liability on a broad range of parties, known as Potentially Responsible Parties (PRPs). These PRPs include past and current property owners, as well as parties involved in the transportation or disposal of hazardous substances (Weissman and Sowinski Jr, 2015). Sites subject to CERCLA cleanups are listed on the National Priorities List (NPL) and are commonly referred to as NPL sites.RCRA, enacted in 1976, grants the EPA the authority to regulate hazardous waste. Initially, RCRA focused on the management of hazardous waste at operating facilities, with limited provisions for environmental cleanups. However, after 1984, RCRA’s cleanup provisions, known as corrective actions, became more comprehensive, aligning more closely with those of CERCLA (Stoll, 1990). For example, the 1984 amendments expanded corrective actions to cover all hazardous waste releases, regardless of when the waste was disposed. Like CERCLA, RCRA’s corrective action also empowers the EPA to require owners and operators to remediate contaminated sites.While both CERCLA and RCRA impose cleanup costs on landowners, an important distinction is that CERCLA has a broader jurisdiction (Stoll, 1990).2 Specifically, CERCLA governs the cleanup of any site contaminated by hazardous substances, whereas RCRA’s corrective actions affect mainly facilities that treat, store, dispose of hazardous waste, commonly known as TSDFs (Bromm, 1998, Eisen, 1996, Burnham-Howard, 2004).3 The importance of this distinction becomes apparent in Section 2.3, where I describe how it is used to construct the treated and control groups.When CERCLA and RCRA overlap, they often serve as substitutes, as cleanups under either statute typically result in similar outcomes. The EPA generally prefers to use RCRA when a facility is subject to both statutes (EPA, 2011).4 If, during the assessment of a potentially contaminated site, the EPA determines that RCRA corrective action can be used to enforce cleanup, it would generally not add the site to the NPL list (Fig. A.1).The cleanup liability imposed by these statutes is significant. The average cleanup cost for a contaminated site is approximately $43 million (Greenstone and Gallagher, 2008), with costs for larger, more complex sites reaching billions of dollars (Blair, 2011, Ohlrogge, 2024). Cleanup costs are also difficult to estimate (Eisen, 1996). On average, the evaluation phase alone takes about a decade, and the cleanup itself requires an additional ten years (GAO, 1997). Furthermore, cleanup liability is joint and several, meaning that any single party can be held responsible for the entire cleanup cost, regardless of their specific contribution to the contamination.Because purchasers assume ownership after acquiring land, they face substantial and uncertain remedial costs (Witkin, 2004). As a result, environmental liability imposed by the two statutes significantly hinders the transaction of industrial property (Bromm, 1998, Burnham-Howard, 2004). This barrier has led to the abandonment of many “potentially” contaminated industrial sites, commonly referred to as brownfields (Higgins, 2001, Bromm, 1998, Weissman and Sowinski Jr, 2015). According to the EPA, there are currently approximately 450,000 brownfields in the United States.2.2. Small business liability relief and Brownfields Revitalization ActTo address the brownfields problem, Congress enacted the Small Business Liability Relief and Brownfields Revitalization Act (Brownfields Act hereafter) in 2002, which introduced two significant changes to purchaser liability.First, the Brownfields Act clarified the “innocent landowner”(ILO) defense, an existing liability defense that absolves purchasers from cleanup liability if they had no reason to know about past contamination. To successfully invoke this defense, a purchaser must demonstrate that she did not discover any contamination during due diligence prior to the purchase. However, before the Brownfields Act, the effectiveness of this defense was undermined by uncertainty about the due diligence requirements (Beless, 1997). The Act resolved this by establishing clear standards for environmental assessment (Meltz, 2002).Second, the reform introduced the new “bona fide prospective purchaser (BFPP)” defense. Unlike the ILO defense, the BFPP defense protects the purchaser from cleanup liability if they are aware of the release or potential release of a hazardous substance on the property (Meltz, 2002). This provision plays a crucial role in facilitating property transactions by reducing the environmental risks associated with acquiring contaminated or potentially contaminated land. To qualify for the BFPP defense, the transaction must have occurred after January 11th, 2002, and purchasers must comply with the criteria outlined in the statute, including conducting a pre-acquisition environmental assessment and appropriately managing hazardous substances after the purchase. Internet Appendix A describes these requirements in more detail.In addition to providing liability protection for potential purchasers, the Act also offers liability relief to small businesses and introduces grants to assess and clean up brownfields (Collins, 2002). However, these provisions are unlikely to affect the interpretation of my results. First, I analyze facilities owned by large public firms. Second, the grants are relatively small, with a maximum of $200,000, which limits their potential effect on emissions. Finally, there is no reason to believe that firm size and the likelihood of receiving brownfield grants are correlated with my classification of the treated and control groups.2.3. Measuring exposure to the Brownfields ActThis section outlines the construction of treated and control groups. I begin with my primary treatment measure, which classifies plants based on their exposure to RCRA regulation. I also develop three alternative treatment measures designed to address potential weaknesses of the primary measure.2.3.1. Main exposure measuresA key aspect of the Brownfields Act is that it offers liability relief from CERCLA to purchasers but does not extend these protections to RCRA liability (EPA, 2022).5 Consequently, plants subject solely to CERCLA are more significantly impacted, as their purchasers benefit from liability protections without concern for RCRA liabilities. In contrast, plants subject to both CERCLA and RCRA serve as a natural comparison group, as RCRA liabilities continue to pose barriers to land acquisition even after the reform (Burnham-Howard, 2004).I exploit this statutory distinction to construct the treated and control groups. Since CERCLA cleanups apply to any contaminated plants, while RCRA’s corrective actions primarily target plants involved in the treatment, storage, or disposal of hazardous waste (TSDFs), I classify the treated and control groups based on whether plants qualify as TSDFs using the EPA’s RCRAinfo database. The specific steps are outlined below, with additional details provided in Internet Appendix B.Given that TSDFs must demonstrate their ability to safely manage hazardous waste by obtaining RCRA operating permits, I first identify TSDFs using the RCRA permits database (PM_EVENT). Next, I use the handler dataset (HD_HANDLER), which includes a flag for plants that handle hazardous waste, to identify additional TSDFs. Lastly, I use the Biennial Hazardous Waste Report, which has been available since 2001 and requires plants generating large volumes of hazardous waste to report the quantities treated, disposed of, or recycled on site. I classify plants as TSDFs if they report handling hazardous waste on site.6 Since TSDF status may be endogenous (i.e., influenced by the reform), I use pre-reform information, including the 2001 Biennial Report and permit events that occurred before 2002, to classify plants into treated and control groups.After identifying the TSDFs, I match this information with plants in my primary dataset. Treated plants are those not classified as TSDFs (i.e., plants likely regulated by CERCLA only), while the control group consists of the remaining plants (i.e., plants more likely regulated by CERCLA and RCRA). To measure treatment at the firm level, I classify a firm as treated if the majority of its plants were treated one year before the Brownfields Act.Fig. 1 plots the location of non-TSDFs (Treated) and TSDFs (Control) plants. The figure demonstrates that both types of plants are similarly distributed nationwide. Given the substantial geographic variation in environmental regulations, this suggests that the treated and control groups are likely subject to comparable regulatory environments.It is important to recognize potential sources of measurement error when relying on TSDF status to identify treated and control groups. One potential source of error arises from the use of the Biennial Hazardous Waste Report, which only became publicly available after 2001. Plants managing hazardous waste on site would still be subject to RCRA cleanups, even if they ceased handling hazardous waste prior to 2001. As a result, there is a possibility that such ex-TSDFs are misclassified as non-TSDFs. However, this measurement error is likely minimal for several reasons.Download: Download high-res image (817KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. Location of TSDF vs. Non-TSDF Plants. The figure plots the location of Non-TSDF (Treated; Red dots) and TSDF (Control; Blue dots) in the United States. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)First, as previously explained, in addition to the Biennial Hazardous Waste Report, I rely on TSDF permit and handler information, which predates 2001 and captures many facilities with former TSDF status. Second, plants classified as non-TSDFs incur nearly zero RCRA cleanups (see Panel B of Table 3).Measurement error may also arise from the fact that RCRA corrective actions can apply to non-TSDFs under three distinct conditions. First, corrective actions may be imposed if contamination poses an imminent and substantial endangerment to health or the environment, as specified in RCRA Section 7003. Second, they may apply to facilities contaminated by hazardous waste released from a neighboring TSDF, under the authority of RCRA Section 3008(h). Third, states authorized to enforce RCRA may impose corrective actions more broadly, extending them to non-TSDFs.72.3.2. Alternative exposure measuresTo address these concerns, I implement three alternative treatment intensity measures. The first method leverages variations in environmental liability across states. In addition to CERCLA, which is administered by the federal government, all states impose their own environmental liabilities on purchasers (Sigman, 2010). Similar to RCRA, these state-level liabilities continue to deter property transactions even after the Brownfields Act (Burnham-Howard, 2004). Consequently, plants in states with weaker environmental liability regimes are more significantly impacted by the Brownfields Act, as purchasers benefit from reduced CERCLA liability while facing lower risks from state-imposed environmental obligations.I measure the stringency of state-level environmental liability based on whether states adopt joint and several liability regimes, as determined by a 2001 survey conducted by the Environmental Law Institute. Under joint and several liability, any party can be held responsible for the entire cleanup cost, regardless of their specific contribution to the contamination. Fig. 2 illustrates the states with and without joint and several liability. This approach is grounded in previous research that indicates that such regimes reduce property values and deter transactions (Sigman, 2010).Second, I implement an industry-level treatment intensity measure, classifying a firm as treated if it operates in an industry with a significant number of treated plants. This method permits treatment designation even when plants are not explicitly categorized as non-TSDFs.Download: Download high-res image (293KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 2. State Liability: Joint and Several. This figure highlights states with and without joint and several liability regimes, with states operating under a joint and several liability regime shaded.The final approach measures exposure based on the thickness of the local real estate market. The idea is that reducing purchasers’ liability would have a more substantial effect on plants with more potential buyers. Given that firms typically sell property to neighboring industry peers (Bernstein et al., 2019), I define a plant as affected if a large number of its industry peers (top decile) are located in the same county. Fig. 3 highlights counties with thick real estate market for plants operating in the most common industries in my sample: Chemical Manufacturing (Pane A), Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing (Panel B), and Transportation Equipment Manufacturing (Panel C).The figure reveals some overlap between the industries but also notable differences. For example, while counties in southern California and Arizona are thick markets for all three industries, counties in Pennsylvania exhibit significantly thicker markets for Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing compared to the other two industries.Download: Download high-res image (2MB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 3. article.3. Economic predictions: The Brownfields Act’s effect on emissions is theoretically ambiguous. On one hand, exempting purchasers from cleanup liability may reduce emissions by increasing landowners’ net worth. In the U.S., limited liability protections allow firms to discharge environmental and other debts through bankruptcy. These protections distort the behavior of firms in hazardous industries, incentivizing them to take excessive environmental and public health risks, knowing that liabilities can be avoided in the event of insolvency (Shavell, 1986, Boomhower, 2019). By reducing purchaser liability, the reform mitigates such risk-shifting incentives by enhancing landowners’ net worth. Several economic forces explain how a reduction in purchaser liability positively impacts net worth:First, reducing purchaser liability increases the demand for industrial land by lowering the perceived environmental risks associated with its acquisition. This heightened demand raises real estate prices (Sigman, 2010, Howland, 2000, McGrath, 2000), directly boosting the net worth of current landowners.Second, greater demand improves the liquidity of industrial land, enabling firms to allocate resources more efficiently. In thin asset markets, firms may be forced to retain unproductive assets or miss opportunities to acquire more productive ones. Improved liquidity facilitates the efficient reallocation of assets across firms, enhancing productivity and profitability (Gavazza, 2011, Bernstein et al., 2019, Shleifer and Vishny, 1992, Hsieh and Klenow, 2009). Additionally, enhanced liquidity increases the reversibility of investments in industrial land. Knowing that land can be redeployed in the future, firms are more inclined to invest in production equipment ex-ante, thereby increasing land utilization rates and firm value.Finally, reducing purchaser liability increases the liquidation value of land, enabling firms to pledge assets with higher collateral value. This reduces lenders’ risk in the event of borrower default, thereby lowering the cost of debt and enhancing firm value (Williamson, 1988, Shleifer and Vishny, 1992, Benmelech et al., 2005, Cvijanović, 2014, Benmelech and Bergman, 2009).The reform could also reduce emissions through a liability-shifting mechanism: as purchaser liability decreases, a greater share of the cleanup burden shifts to current landowners, incentivizing them to reduce pollution. Unlike the net worth channel, this mechanism lowers current landowners’ net worth, as increased environmental risk leads to a higher cost of capital and hinders landowners’ ability to obtain financing.Conversely, the reform may increase emissions by enabling treated plants to be sold more easily, even when contaminated. This could encourage current landowners to pollute more, knowing the cleanup burden can be shifted to future purchasers exempt from liability.
article.4. Data and research design: 4.1. DataMy main analysis utilizes data from the EPA’s Toxic Release Inventory (TRI) Program, which requires annual reporting on waste management practices by U.S. facilities.8 Despite being self-reported, the EPA conducts audits to ensure the accuracy of TRI data (Akey and Appel, 2021, Xu and Kim, 2022, Bellon, 2024b). TRI facilities report the quantity of pollutants released into the air, water, and ground for each covered chemical. Ground pollution includes releases into underground injection wells, landfills, and surface impoundments. Air pollution is categorized into stack emissions (e.g., emissions from confined vents, pipes, and ducts) and fugitive emissions (e.g., evaporative losses). Surface water emissions include discharges into bodies of water such as streams, rivers, lakes, and oceans. To quantify a plant’s total emissions, I aggregate the air, water, and ground emissions into a single metric (hereafter referred to as toxic emissions). According to the EPA, this aggregated measure is the most reliable proxy for overall pollution.In addition to emissions, TRI facilities report their pollution abatement efforts in the EPA’s P2 database. These activities are reported at the chemical level and classified into eight broad categories, as detailed in Table A.2. To measure investment in pollution abatement, I focus on the most prevalent abatement activities as in previous work (Bellon, 2024b, Akey and Appel, 2021, Naaraayanan et al., 2020): namely, spill and leak prevention, good operating practices, and process modifications. These efforts are measured both at the intensive margin (number of abatement activities) and at the extensive margin (whether abatement activity is reported).I use two measures of production. The first is the production ratio from the EPA’s P2 database. This ratio is defined as the current year’s output divided by the previous year’s output. For example, if a chemical is used in the production of refrigerators, the production ratio is equal to #ofrefrigeratorsproducedt#ofrefrigeratorsproducedt−1.9 Following Akey and Appel (2021), I exclude extreme production ratios greater than 5 or less than 0 to mitigate the impact of potential reporting errors.10The second measure is Normalized Production, which normalizes production to one in the first year a chemical is reported in the sample and then multiplies forward each year. Specifically, normalized production is computed as: (1)Norm.Productionp,c,t=∏t≠1t1×QuantityProducedp,c,tQuantityProducedp,c,t−1=∏t≠1t1×ProductionRatiop,c,tIn some instances a plant may file multiple forms for the same chemical within a single year, resulting in multiple observations per plant-chemical-year. This often occurs because the plant has several economic units or because of uncertainties in the chemical composition (e.g., “mixtures”). Based on guidance from EPA staff, I aggregate these multiple observations to address this issue.11I obtain firm-level financial variables from Compustat. I use Altman’s Z-score to measure bankruptcy risk, a commonly used measure in the finance literature (e.g., Akey and Appel, 2021, Eisdorfer, 2008, Pedersen, 2019, Gilje, 2016). I use Tobin’s Q to measure firm value, and total assets to proxy for firm size. As in Nini et al. (2012), I use book value of equity scaled by total assets to measure net worth. I merge Compustat with TRI data by matching historical parent company names reported in TRI with those in CRSP, Compustat, and 10-X headers.12 In cases where a parent name is missing for one or two years but present in surrounding years, I impute the missing parents based on available data.I use plant-level data from the National Establishment Time Series (NETS) to measure employment and the transaction of industrial real estate. The NETS database, maintained by Dun & Bradstreet and marketed by Wall and Associates, provides valuable information on plants (establishments), including address, number of employees, legal status, parent company, founding year, and relocation history. I match NETS data with Compustat using the parent names of each plant.13 I merge NETS with TRI using the dunsnumber of each plant.Given that the Brownfields Act aims to increase the liquidity of abandoned properties, I focus on property sales following plant closures and relocations. One advantage of the NETS dataset is that it covers nearly all plants in the U.S., allowing me to track whether a physical location is occupied after a closure or relocation by matching addresses across plants. The details of this process are explained in Internet Appendix C. I define a property transaction as the appearance of new occupants at the same location within a year of a plant’s closure or relocation.In addition to the plant-level divestiture measure, I also measure asset sales at the firm level using the Securities Data Company (SDC) Platinum database. I match the SDC data with Compustat using the linking table provided by Ewens et al. (2024).14 My analysis focuses on divestitures where the acquirer obtains a majority stake in the target, and on divestitures in industries (measured by 4-digit SIC codes) that overlap with those reported in the TRI database.I obtain environmental enforcement data from the EPA’s Enforcement and Compliance History Online (ECHO) database and the RCRAinfo data-base. These databases offer detailed information on regulatory enforcement actions pertaining to environmental statutes, including those related to CERCLA and RCRA corrective actions.4.2. Summary statisticsTable 1 presents the descriptive statistics for the dataset. Continuous variables are winsorized at the 1st and 99th percentiles to mitigate the influence of extreme observations. The main sample comprises 279,284 plant-chemical observations, covering 498 unique chemicals and 9,970 unique plants owned by 1,012 firms. Of these, 7,592 plants and 867 firms belong to the treated group, defined using the TSDF status of plants. The average plant emits approximately 35.65 thousand pounds of toxic pollutants per chemical annually. Within the sample, 2.6% of plants report investments in abatement efforts aimed at preventing spills and leaks. Additionally, 5.7% of plants have implemented abatement efforts related to good operating practices, and 3.3% have modified their processes to reduce emissions. The average production ratio is 0.96.The typical firm has a Z-score of 3.2, a Tobin’s Q of 1.6, a leverage ratio of 0.32, and total assets amounting to $6.1 billion. Approximately 16.6% of firms report at least one divestiture annually. The average plant employs 471 people, with a 1.4% probability of closure and a 0.9% probability of relocation. On average, 0.8% of plants sell their real estate through closure and relocation each year.Table 1. Summary statistics. Balancing testTable 2 compares pre-Brownfields Act characteristics between treated and control firms. Panel A reports results using my primary treatment measure (TSDF status). The findings show that treated and control firms exhibit no significant differences in emissions, production, Z-score, Tobin’s Q, leverage, or total assets. Both groups also demonstrate similar propensities to invest in spill and leak prevention technologies and to modify production processes to reduce pollution. However, treated firms display higher levels of operational-related abatement activities, potentially due to their marginally higher net worth, which may incentivize greater pollution reduction efforts. While observing similar characteristics between treated and control groups before the reform are not a necessary condition for identification in a difference-in-differences framework, these similarities enhance the credibility of a causal interpretation of the results.The remaining panels repeat this analysis for alternative treatment measures. Panel B defines treatment based on whether firms have a majority of their plants in states without joint and several liability. The results indicate that, while treated and control groups show no differences in most characteristics, they differ in certain critical dimensions. Specifically, there is some evidence that the treated group emits more toxic chemicals compared to the control group, although the difference is not statistically significant. This is consistent with the notion that firms operating in states with weaker liability regimes face lower environmental penalties and, consequently, emit more toxic chemicals. Treated firms also have fewer assets, which may further contribute to their higher emissions.In Panel C, firms are classified into treated and control groups based on the thickness of the real estate markets in which they operate. The results reveal pre-reform differences in emissions, with treated firms emitting fewer toxic chemicals than control firms. This difference may be attributed to the characteristics of thick real estate markets, which are often more populous. Higher population densities attract greater regulatory scrutiny, likely exposing treated firms to increased environmental oversight, thereby reducing toxic emissions.Combined, these results demonstrate the advantage of using TSDF status to classify treated and control groups compared to location-based measures. Environmental regulations vary significantly across locations, leading to substantial differences in environmental activity. In contrast, TSDFs and non-TSDFs are more evenly distributed across the United States, resulting in a more homogeneous classification of treated and control groups.Table 2. Balance test. Research designTo assess the impact of purchaser liability on toxic emissions, I employ a difference-in-differences (DiD) framework. Given the skewed and left-censored nature of emission data, I estimate a Poisson model, as suggested by Cohn et al. (2022): (2)yc,p,i,t=β⋅Treatedi(p)×Postt+αpc+αct+αi+ϵc,p,i,tHere, yc,p,i,t represents the pounds of toxic chemical c emitted by plant p operated by parent firm i in year t. I consider measuring treatment at both the firm and plant levels. Treatedi is a binary indicator set to 1 for treated firms and 0 otherwise. I use a firm-level treatment variable because an increase in a firm’s net worth affects emissions at all of its plants. Treatedp is an indicator for treated plants. Postt is an indicator variable that equals 1 for years after the reform (i.e., from 2002 onward). I include plant×chemical fixed effects (αp,c) to control for time-invariant factors at plant-chemical levels, and include chemical×year fixed effects (αc,t) to enable comparisons of emissions for the same chemical in the same year between the treated and control groups. This helps address the challenge of comparing emissions across different chemicals and accounts for the changing reporting requirements for these chemicals. Firm fixed effects (αi) are included to control for any time-invariant firm characteristics. Note that Treatedi, Treatedp, and Postt are absorbed by the firm, plant×chemical, and chemical×year fixed effects, respectively.In more stringent specifications, I introduce industry×year fixed effects to control for industry-level shocks that may correlate with emissions.15 Additionally, I include state×year fixed effects to account for differences in environmental regulations across states (Burnham-Howard, 2004, Eisen, 1996). The coefficient of interest, β, captures the effect of the Brownfields Act on toxic emissions. When using a firm (plant) level treatment variable, standard errors are clustered at the firm (plant) level.
article.5. Validations: 5.1. EnforcementsI begin by conducting validation tests using enforcement data from the EPA’s ECHO and RCRAinfo databases. The detailed steps for constructing the sample used in these tests are described in Internet Appendix D.My first validation test examines whether the EPA is more likely to address contamination at treated plants using CERCLA rather than RCRA. Given that the Brownfields Act exempts purchasers from CERCLA liability, higher exposure to CERCLA enforcement at treated plants compared to control plants would suggest a greater decline in liability for purchasers post-reform. To test this, I estimate the following linear probability model: (3)yp,t=β⋅Treatedp+ϵp,tWhere yp,t is an indicator variable set to 1 if plant p is subject to CERCLA enforcement action in year t and 0 otherwise. Treatedp is an indicator variable equal to 1 for treated plants and 0 otherwise.Panel A of Table 3 reports the result. Column (1) shows that the EPA is 67.2% more likely to be addressed through CERCLA compared to control plants. This difference is economically significant, representing an increase comparable to the sample mean of 65.7%. To account for regional discretion in EPA enforcement decisions, I include EPA region-by-year fixed effects in Column (2). In Column (3), I further control for time-varying industry shocks. Across these specifications, the point estimates remain stable, ranging from 0.566 to 0.573, and are statistically significant at the 1% level.Next, I examine the average number of CERCLA and RCRA cleanups incurred by treated and control plants. If RCRA cleanups primarily affect control plants (or TSDFs), we would expect two outcomes: (1) control plants (TSDFs) should experience significantly more RCRA cleanups than treated plants (non-TSDFs), and (2) treated plants should exhibit minimal or no RCRA cleanups. The results, reported in Panel B of Table 3, confirm these expectations. Specifically, while treated and control plants exhibit similar numbers of CERCLA cleanups, control plants incur substantially more RCRA cleanups. The average control plant incurred 0.641 RCRA cleanups, whereas treated plants experienced only 0.045. This difference is statistically significant at the 1% level. The near-zero exposure to RCRA among treated plants supports the validity of my treated and control group classification in two ways. First, it suggests that the redeployment of treated plants is unlikely to be hindered by RCRA liability. Second, it indicates that the misclassification of TSDFs as non-TSDFs is minimal.Table 3. DivestituresThe second validation test examines whether treated plants experience an increase in divestiture following the reform. This test is motivated by prior research showing that reducing purchaser liability facilitates real estate transactions (Boyd et al., 1996, McGrath, 2000, Howland, 2000, Schoenbaum, 2002, Sigman, 2010). To explore this, I estimate the following plant-level difference-in-differences model: (4)yp,i,c,t=β⋅Treatedp×Postt+αp+αl,t+ϵp,i,c,tWhere yp,i,t is an indicator that equals 1 if plant p sold its real estate through closure or relocation in year t, and 0 otherwise. I include plant fixed effects (αp) to control for time-invariant plant characteristics (e.g., plant size), and county×year fixed effects (αlt) to control for time-varying local economic characteristics that could influence real estate transactions (Bernstein et al., 2019). The inclusion of plant and county×year fixed effects subsume Treatedp and Postt, respectively. Standard errors are clustered at the plant level.Panel A of Table 4 reports the results. Column (1) provides the baseline specification. The point estimate suggests that treated plants experience a 1.1 percentage point increase in the probability of selling real estate post-reform compared to control plants. This increase is economically meaningful, as it is slightly larger than the unconditional probability of selling property (0.8%). The magnitudes remain unchanged when firm- and industry-year fixed effects are added in columns (2) and (3), respectively.In panel B of Table 4, I perform a similar analysis, but at the firm level. The outcome variable is an indicator of whether a firm reported a divestiture in year t. For this analysis, Treatedi indicates treated firms. The results in column (1) show that treated firms experience a 4.4 percentage point increase in the likelihood of asset sales post-reform relative to control firms. This corresponds to a 27% increase compared to the sample mean. These findings remain robust when including state×year and industry×year fixed effects.Overall, the findings in Table 3 and Table 4 validate my empirical design by demonstrating that treated plants are more exposed to the reform, as evidenced by their greater exposure to CERCLA and increased divestiture compared to the control group post-reform.Table 4. article.6. Main results: 6.1. Toxic emissions6.1.1. Main exposure measure: TSDFs vs. non-TSDFsHaving validated the classification of treated and control groups, I now examine the effects of the Brownfields Act on emissions using my primary treatment measure. The findings are presented in Table 5. In columns (1)–(3), I use a firm-level treatment indicator (Treatedi). Column (1) presents the baseline specification, while columns (2) and (3) incorporate industry×year and state×year fixed effects, respectively. Across all specifications, the results show that, in response to the reform, treated firms reduce emissions relative to control firms, with point estimates ranging from −0.071 to −0.081, all statistically significant at the 5% level. The stability of these coefficients across different specifications suggests that the results are unlikely to be driven by unobserved heterogeneity. These estimates correspond to an emissions reduction of between 6.9% (e−0.071−1) and 7.8% (e−0.081−1).In columns (4)–(6), I use the same specifications but with a plant-level treatment indicator (Treatedp) – Although the coefficients are negative, they exhibit weaker economic and statistical significance. The point estimates range from −0.028 to −0.042, with statistical significance only in column (6) and approaching significance in column (4) (p-value = 0.12). This corresponds to a 2.8% (e−0.028−1) to 4.1% (e−0.042−1) decline in emissions by treated plants.In column (7), I include both plant- and firm-level treatment indicators, effectively conducting a horse race between the two. The point estimate for the firm-level treatment indicator interacted with Postt is negative and statistically significant, while the plant-level treatment indicator interacted with Postt becomes statistically insignificant. This result suggests that the reduction in emissions primarily stems from firm-wide emission cuts.While the emission reduction is consistent with both the net worth channel and the liability-shifting mechanism, the fact that the decrease is driven primarily by firm-wide emission cuts lends stronger support to the net worth channel. This is because an increase in firm net worth would reduce emissions across all plants, not just treated ones, whereas the shift in cleanup responsibility to landowners occurs only at treated plants.To assess the timing of the effect, I plot the coefficient dynamics around the reform in Fig. 4. The figure is constructed by replacing Postt with year-specific indicators before and after the Brownfields Act and interacting them with a firm-level treatment indicator. The coefficients in the pre-reform period are stable and indistinguishable from zero, indicating no pre-existing trends. However, following the reform, emissions by treated firms start to decline relative to control firms, with the reduction stabilizing after 2004. These results suggest that the observed decline in emissions is driven by the reform and not by a broader trend unrelated to the Brownfields Act.Table 5. Alternative exposure measureNext, I consider the three alternative methods for measuring exposure to the Brownfields Act. The first method uses variations in environmental liability across states, classifying plants located in states without joint and several liability as treated. The results using this measure are reported in columns (1) and (2) of Table 6. In column (1), I use a continuous exposure measure, %Treated, defined as the fraction of a firm’s plants located in states without joint and several liability standards in 2001. I divide %Treated by its standard deviation to facilitate interpretation of the economic magnitude. The results show that a one-standard-deviation increase in %Treated corresponds to a 2.5% decline in toxic emissions post-reform. In column (2), I employ an indicator variable, Treated, which equals 1 for firms with a majority of their plants in states without joint and several liability standards. Using this measure, I find that treated firms reduce toxic emissions by 7.2% compared to control firms post-2002. Notably, both the sign and magnitude are consistent with the main result, reinforcing confidence in the estimated effects from the main analysis.Second, I measure treatment at the industry level, defining firms as more exposed to the reform if they operate in an industry (3-digit NAICS) with a high proportion of treated plants. Columns (3) and (4) of Table 6 report the results. In column (3), the exposure variable %Treated represents the proportion of plants in an industry affected by the reform. In column (4), a firm is treated if it operates in an industry where the fraction of treated plants is in the top quartile (fraction> 80%). The coefficients are negative and statistically significant, with the economic magnitude in column (2) similar to the main analysis.The final approach measures exposure based on the thickness of the local real estate market. I define a plant as affected if a large number of its industry peers (top decile) are located in the same county. I estimate the baseline specification with this measure in columns (5) and (6). Column (5) uses a continuous measure of exposure, %Treated, defined as the fraction of a firm’s plants located in thick markets. In column (6), a firm is treated if the fraction of its plants in thick markets is in the top quartile (fraction> 63%). The results are consistent with the main findings in terms of economic magnitude. Together, the three approaches confirm that the results are robust to alternative measures of exposure to the Brownfields Act.Table 6. Alternative exposure measures. Abatement activitiesNext, I examine changes in abatement initiatives, which provide an alternative measure of environmental outcomes beyond emissions. According to the EPA, investing in abatement activities is the preferred strategy for managing toxic waste (see Fig. A.2). If the reform reduces emissions by encouraging landowners to mitigate environmental risks, we should expect to see an increase in pollution abatement efforts among affected landowners following the reform.I test this prediction in Table 7. Following previous research (Akey and Appel, 2021, Naaraayanan et al., 2020), I focus on the most common abatement efforts as the primary outcome variables. The dependent variables in the odd-numbered columns indicate whether a specific type of abatement effort was implemented, while the even-numbered columns use the number of reported abatement efforts as the outcome variable. The results indicate that, after 2002, treated firms increased their efforts to prevent spills and leaks relative to control firms. The coefficients in columns (1) and (2) are both positive and statistically significant. The point estimate in column (1) is 0.015, representing a 57.7% increase compared to the sample mean, suggesting a meaningful shift towards preventing environmental disasters.However, there is no significant evidence that treated firms increase their investments in other types of abatement activities, such as process modifications or good operating practices. The point estimates in columns (3) through (6) are small and statistically insignificant. To the extent that investments in spill and leak prevention better reflect efforts to reduce risk compared to other abatement strategies, these results align with the notion that the reform reduces emissions by mitigating landowners’ risk-taking incentives.Table 7. Additional testsIn this section, I perform additional tests to address potential concerns with my main findings. A plausible concern is that the results might be driven by a secular decline in emissions due to technological progress or increased regulation, which could vary in intensity for larger or smaller emitters. If the treated and control group have different pre-reform emissions, the observed decline in emissions after 2002 could reflect this broader trend rather than the impact of the Brownfields Act. While the no-pre-trend result in Fig. 4 suggests that this is unlikely the case, I further address this issue by controlling for changes in pollution trends based on pre-reform emission levels by adding Toxic Emissionpre×Post to the regressions in Table 5. Toxic Emissionpre represents the average emissions by plant p for chemical c before 2002. The results reported in Table O.A.1 show that the main findings remain robust under this specification. Importantly, the point estimates for Treated×Post remain largely unchanged, indicating that differential pollution trends among high- and low-emitters do not explain the results.Another concern is that my main findings could be driven by contemporaneous industry shocks that differentially impact emissions of treated and control firms. To evaluate this possibility, I conduct placebo tests by randomly assigning treatment status across plants with parent firms in the same industry. For each iteration, I re-estimate the baseline specification and plot the distribution of the resulting coefficients in Fig. 5. If my main findings were driven by contemporaneous industry shocks, these placebo tests would be expected to produce estimates similar to those in my main specification (−0.073). However, the results suggest otherwise: the placebo point estimates are centered around zero (mean =−0.003), and 196 out of 200 estimates are to the right of the coefficient in my main specification (red line). These findings indicate that contemporaneous industry shocks are unlikely to explain my results.Additionally, a potential issue arises from the potential correlation between the two environmental statutes. If the two statutes are highly correlated, the treatment indicator may capture a common environmental risk component, where plants subject to both statutes (control plants) could be larger emitters and thus reduce emissions at a different pace compared to treated plants, complicating the interpretations of my estimates. To assess the extent of this issue, I use data from the EPA’s Enforcement and Compliance History Online (ECHO) and the RCRAinfo database to calculate the correlation between the two statutes. The results are reported in Table O.A.2. I find that the correlation between the two statutes is only 0.03 (Panel A). Additionally, conditional on cleanup enforcement, the correlation between CERCLA and RCRA is −0.96 (Panel B), which is consistent with the notion that once the EPA decides to clean up a site, it relies solely on one statute for enforcement. These results suggest that overlap between the two statutes is unlikely to complicate the interpretation of my findings.Download: Download high-res image (184KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 5. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)Next, to ensure the results are not sensitive to how treated firms are defined, I vary the threshold for treatment classification. Table O.A.3 reports the findings. In column (1), a firm is classified as treated if at least 40% of its plants are affected. In columns (2) and (3), the threshold for treated firms is raised to 60% and 70%, respectively. To ensure a meaningful comparison between firms with a high proportion of affected plants and those with a low proportion, I exclude firms with a median level of affected plants — specifically, those with 40% to 60% affected plants in column (2) and 40% to 70% in column (3). Across all specifications, the interaction term Treated×Post consistently yields negative and statistically significant coefficients, indicating that the results remain robust to variations in the thresholds used to define treated firms.My main specification addresses the skewness of emissions by estimating a Poisson model. As an alternative, I estimate an OLS model with a scaled outcome variable in Table O.A.4. The validity of this method depends critically on the use of appropriate scaling variables that accurately capture plants’ exposure to toxic emissions, as noise in the scaling variable can bias the estimates (Cohn et al., 2022). The natural scaling variable in this context is the pounds of toxic waste managed, as a plant’s emissions for a given chemical are directly proportional to the volume of waste it manages for that chemical. Thus, in column (1), I use emissions scaled by toxic waste managed as the outcome variable. Because emissions should also be proportional to economic activity, I scale emissions by production and employment in columns (2) and (3), respectively. Across all specifications, the coefficient on Treated×Post is negative and statistically significant, demonstrating that the results are robust to this alternative approach for addressing skewness in the outcome variable.In addition to the above tests, I conduct several other robustness tests which I summarize here and provide details in Internet Appendix E. I show that the findings are not affected by changes in the list of chemicals and industries reported in the TRI database (Table O.A.5). The results also hold when I cluster standard errors differently (Table O.A.6) and collapse the sample to address serial correlation (Table O.A.7). Finally, the estimated effects remain robust when expanding or contracting the sample period (Table O.A.8) and when excluding the smallest plants, which are more prone to misreporting emissions (Table O.A.9).
article.7. The channel: Previous results suggest that the reform reduces emissions and increases abatement efforts, particularly among treated firms. As discussed in Section 6.1, this outcome is more consistent with the net worth channel than with the liability-shifting mechanism. An increase in net worth affects emissions across all plants, while the shift in cleanup responsibility applies only to treated plants. In this section, I conduct additional tests to further disentangle the mechanisms underlying my main findings.7.1. Net worthThe net worth channel predicts that the net worth of affected firms should increase following the reform, whereas the liability-shifting mechanism would result in increased liabilities, leading to a decrease in net worth.To test these predictions, I estimate the following OLS model: (5)yi,t=βTreatedi×Postt+αi+αt+ϵi,tHere, yi,t represents the empirical proxies of net worth. Treatedi is an indicator of whether the firm is treated, and zero otherwise. Postt indicates the post-reform period. The inclusion of firm fixed effects subsumes Treatedi, and year fixed effects subsumes Postt. I use three measures of net worth, with the findings reported in Table 8.My first measure of net worth is the Altman’s Z-score, which is inversely related to the risk of bankruptcy. Columns (1)–(3) present the results. Column (1) estimates the baseline specification, while column (2) introduces industry×year fixed effects, and column (3) adds state×year fixed effects. The point estimates range between 0.433 and 0.514, and are statistically significant at the 5% level. These results indicate that the Z-score of treated firms increased relative to control firms after 2002. The increase is economically significant, implying an increase of 13.4% to 15.9% compared to the sample mean.My second measure of net worth is the book value of equity scaled by total assets. Columns (4)–(6) report the findings. The coefficients indicate that, following the reform, the net worth of treated firms increased by 0.026 compared to that of the control firms. The standard deviation of this outcome variable is 0.254. Thus, the effect represents 10.2% of the standard deviation of net worth.Lastly, I use Tobin’s Q to measure firm value. Columns (7)–(9) show that treated firms experienced an increase in Tobin’s Q relative to control firms after the reform, with point estimates ranging from 0.155 to 0.177. This increase is equivalent to 9.8% to 11.2% of the sample mean.These findings indicate that the reform positively impacts the net worth of industrial firms, consistent with the net worth channel.Table 8. Cross-sectional heterogeneityNext, I investigate whether the decrease in emissions varies based on the extent to which firms internalize the cost of pollution. Firms in poor financial health may not internalize the full cost of pollution because they can discharge environmental liabilities through bankruptcy (Akey and Appel, 2021, Bellon and Boualam, 2024, Ohlrogge, 2023). Similarly, smaller companies tend to engage in riskier environmental practices because they do not internalize cleanup costs that exceed the value of their assets (Shavell, 1986, Boomhower, 2019). I refer to these firms as having strong pollution-shifting incentives. According to the net worth mechanism, firms with strong pollution-shifting incentives are expected to reduce emissions the most, as the reform alleviates these incentives. In contrast, the liability-shifting mechanism should have minimal effects on such firms, as they can discharge the redirected purchaser liability through bankruptcy.To test these opposing predictions, I segment the sample based on measures of pollution-shifting incentives and re-estimate the baseline specification. Additionally, I employ a triple-differences framework as an alternative approach: (6)yc,p,i,t=β1Treatedi×Postt+β2Treatedi×Postt×Proxyi+αProxy,t+αp,c+αc,t+αi+ϵc,p,i,tHere, Proxyi is an indicator equal to 1 if a firm has high pollution-shifting incentives and 0 otherwise.16 The inclusion of high pollution-shifting×year fixed effects (αproxy,t) controls for any time-varying shocks that have a differential effect on firms with high and low pollution-shifting incentives. Both Proxyi and Proxyi×Postt are subsumed by αproxy,t, while Proxyi×Treatedi is subsumed by firm fixed effects. The coefficient of interest, β2, measures the differential effect of the reform on firms with high versus low pollution-shifting incentives. I employ four distinct measures to capture these motives.The first measure is financial distress, proxied by the Altman Z-score. I classify a firm as distressed if its average Z-score before the reform is below the sample median, and non-distressed otherwise. Panel A of Table 9 presents the result. I find that the decline in emissions is driven by firms with higher bankruptcy risk. The estimated effect of the Brownfields Act is −0.118 and statistically significant at the 5% level for the distressed sample (column (1)), while it is positive and statistically insignificant for the non-distressed sample (column (2)). In the triple-differences specification (column (3)), the coefficient on Treated×Post×Proxy is −0.140, indicating that distressed firms reduce emissions more than non-distressed firms following the reform.The second measure is firm size, proxied by total assets. I define a firm as small if its average total assets before 2002 is below the sample median and as large otherwise. Panel B reports the results. I find that the reduction in emissions is driven primarily by small firms. The effect of the Brownfields Act on emissions is −0.155 for small firms (column (1)) and −0.007 for large firms (column(2)). In column (3), the triple interaction term is negative and statistically significant at the 10% level, indicating that small firms respond to the Brownfield Act by cutting emissions more aggressively than large firms.The third measure is contingent environmental liabilities, obtained from Akey and Appel (2021).17 The authors collect information on whether a firm disclosed an accrual for future remedial costs in its 1997 financial statements. The logic behind this measure is two-fold. First, the disclosure of contingent environmental liabilities reveals that the firm tends to engage in risky environmental practices. Second, all else being equal, firms with higher contingent environmental liabilities are closer to default and, therefore, more prone to pollution-shifting.18 Panel C reports the findings using this measure. The results indicate that the decrease in emissions is concentrated among firms with contingent environmental liabilities. The coefficient of the triple interaction is negative and statistically significant, demonstrating that treated firms with such liabilities reduce their emissions by 20.5% more than those without.The final measure is the Paydex score from the NETS database, which reflects the financial health of plants. Lower Paydex scores indicate worse financial health and higher risk of solvency. Panel D reports the results. Columns (1) and (2) show that the effect is driven by plants with low Paydex scores. In column (3), the coefficient on the triple-interaction term is negative but not statistically significant at conventional levels (p-value = 0.168). This is likely due to the reduced sample size caused by missing Paydex score data. Overall, these cross-sectional tests indicate that the effect is most pronounced in cases where the net worth effect is expected to have the greatest impact.Table 9. Economic activityIn my next set of analyses, I examine the effects of the reform on economic activity. Real property often serves as collateral for debt financing, and an increase in real estate prices can alleviate financing constraints, thereby stimulating economic activity (Ersahin and Irani, 2020, Chaney et al., 2012). Additionally, improved liquidity in the land market can enhance productivity by facilitating a more efficient allocation of resources (Gavazza, 2011, Hsieh and Klenow, 2009). Since the net worth channel is driven by an increase in real estate value and liquidity, observing a rise in economic activity would lend support to this channel. Conversely, if the observed reduction in emissions is accompanied by a decline in economic activity, this would indicate the liability-shifting channel drives my main findings. Under the liability-shifting mechanism, the transfer of purchaser liability to landowners raises their liability risk, which could increase their cost of capital and reduce economic activity.To assess this, I use two measures of production from the EPA to measure economic activity: the production ratio and the (log of) normalized production. Columns (1)–(6) of Table 10 reports the results. The outcome variable in columns (1)–(3) is the production ratio. The estimates show that the production ratio of treated firms increased by 2.6% to 3.6% relative to control firms after the reform. Columns (4)–(6) present the results for normalized production. The point estimates are similar to those in the first three columns but only statistically significant in one of the specifications.I also use employment data from NETS to measure economic activity. For this analysis, I replace plant×chemical with plant fixed effects and chemical ×year fixed effects with year fixed effects because the analysis is carried out at the plant level. The outcome variable is the log of plant employment. The findings are reported in columns (7)–(9). The estimates indicate that the employment of the treated firm increased by approximately 13% compared to the control firms. However, these estimates should be interpreted with caution, as they are imprecise and statistically significant in only two of the three specifications.Taken together, these findings suggest that the reform has a modest but positive effect on production and employment, consistent with the view that an increase in property value drives the reduction in emissions.Table 10. Economic magnitudesIn my final set of tests, I assess whether the increase in net worth is substantial enough to account for the observed decline in emissions. Specifically, the estimated elasticity between emissions and the Altman Z score in Fig. 6, implies that a one-unit increase in the Z score is associated with a 17% decrease in emissions. Given the estimated increase in the Z-score post-reform ranges from 0.433 and 0.514, this implies a 7.4% to 8.7% decrease in toxic emissions.19 This range closely matches the estimated 7% reduction in emissions.However, this back-of-the-envelope calculation has some limitations. For instance, the relationship between the Z-score and emissions may not be linear; as companies approach bankruptcy, their emissions might increase exponentially. Furthermore, Ohlrogge (2023) shows that emissions drop significantly after companies file for bankruptcy.20 Keeping these caveats in mind, the calculations provide suggestive evidence that the increase in net worth is sufficient to explain the estimated decline in emissions among treated firms.Overall, the results in this section indicate that the reform reduced emissions when the net worth effect was expected to be most significant and increased the net worth and economic activity of affected firms. These findings provide evidence that the net worth channel is the key mechanism driving the observed reduction in emissions.Download: Download high-res image (163KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 6.
